mnemonics.title: Hazardous E-Waste Elements
mnemonics.content: H-G-P-L-C-B: Mercury (switches), Lead (batteries/CRT), Plastics (BFRs), Lithium (batteries), Cadmium (rechargeable), Barium (CRT)

mnemonics.title: Main E-Waste Categories
mnemonics.content: L-S-I-C-L-E: Large appliances, Small appliances, IT/Communications, Consumer equipment, Lighting, Electrical tools

mnemonics.title: E-Waste Composition
mnemonics.content: F-P-N-G-H: Ferrous metals (25%), Plastics (30%), Non-ferrous (10%), Glass (20%), Hazardous (5%)

mnemonics.title: Heavy Metal Effects
mnemonics.content: N-P-K: Nervous (Lead), Peripheral (Mercury), Kidney (Cadmium) - causes of heavy metals

acronyms.usage: EU directive governing e-waste collection and recycling
Policy approach making manufacturers responsible for end-of-life product management
Products that depend on electric current to function
Chemicals in plastics to prevent fire, problematic in recycling
Common flame retardant in electronics, persistent organic pollutant
Contains most precious metals and hazardous materials
Old monitor/TV technology with high lead content
Modern flat panel display, contains mercury

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pie chart: 30% plastics (large slice), 25% ferrous metals (quarter), 20% glass, 15% circuit boards, 10% other - the glass and circuit boards are the most valuable but hazardous slices
Pyramid structure: Base = bulk materials (plastics, iron), Middle = aluminum/copper, Top = precious metals (gold/silver). As you go up, quantity decreases but value increases exponentially
Households → Businesses → Industries → Government → Healthcare → Each sector is a different pipe feeding into the main e-waste river

analogies.analogy: Like panning for gold in river streams, but instead we're 'panning' through discarded electronics to find precious metals - e-waste is more concentrated than natural ore!
E-waste is like a city - it has iron bones (ferrous), copper nerves (wiring), plastic skin, glass eyes (screens), and toxic blood (hazardous substances) all mixed together
A gold ore mine might yield 5 grams per tonne, but a mobile phone has 200-300 grams per tonne - making our e-waste bins richer mines than natural deposits
Modern electronics have shorter lifespans than mayflies - a phone lasts 2-3 years (like 150 generations of mayflies), while our grandparents' radios lasted 20+ years
